draft.introduction: In Rhetoric I.2, Aristotle defines rhetoric as the faculty of observing the available means of persuasion in each case. He identifies three modes of proof: ethos (character of the speaker), pathos (emotional state of the audience), and logos (the argument itself).

translation.text: Let rhetoric be defined as the faculty of observing in any given case the available means of persuasion. Of the modes of persuasion furnished by the spoken word there are three kinds: the first depends on the personal character of the speaker; the second on putting the audience into a certain frame of mind; the third on the proof, or apparent proof, provided by the words of the speech itself.
translation.original_text: ἔστω δὴ ἡ ῥητορικὴ δύναμις περὶ ἕκαστον τοῦ θεωρῆσαι τὸ ἐνδεχόμενον πιθανόν

translator_notes.note: The triad of ethos, pathos, and logos remains the foundational framework for rhetorical analysis. Unlike Plato, who condemned rhetoric as flattery in the Gorgias, Aristotle treats it as a legitimate counterpart to dialectic—a neutral faculty that can serve truth.
